questions.text: In BitTorrent, what happens to download speed as more users download a popular file - and why?

options.text: Speed decreases - more users compete for the same central server bandwidth
Speed increases - more users have pieces of the file and can upload those pieces to new downloaders simultaneously, creating massive parallel transfer
Speed stays constant - BitTorrent allocates fixed bandwidth per user regardless of swarm size
Speed depends only on the original uploader's connection - other peers don't affect it

questions.explanation: Explanation: BitTorrent inverts the centralised bottleneck completely. In centralised download: 10,000 simultaneous users = each gets 1/10,000 of server bandwidth = very slow. In BitTorrent: 10,000 users = each has some pieces of the file = 10,000 simultaneous upload sources = each new downloader can get pieces from all 10,000 users simultaneously = dramatically faster. The file is split into pieces; any peer with piece #42 can upload it to anyone who needs it. This is why popular torrents (many seeders) download in minutes while unpopular ones (few seeders) can take days or fail entirely. Blockchain applies this same principle: more validators = more resilient and distributed network.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B

Explanation: BitTorrent inverts the centralised bottleneck completely. In centralised download: 10,000 simultaneous users = each gets 1/10,000 of server bandwidth = very slow. In BitTorrent: 10,000 users = each has some pieces of the file = 10,000 simultaneous upload sources = each new downloader can get pieces from all 10,000 users simultaneously = dramatically faster. The file is split into pieces; any peer with piece #42 can upload it to anyone who needs it. This is why popular torrents (many seeders) download in minutes while unpopular ones (few seeders) can take days or fail entirely. Blockchain applies this same principle: more validators = more resilient and distributed network.

questions.text: Wikipedia defeated Microsoft's Encarta despite Encarta having professional editors and massive corporate funding. What was Wikipedia's structural advantage?

options.text: Wikipedia had better technology and a faster website than Encarta
Wikipedia was government-funded and therefore could not be shut down commercially
Wikipedia's decentralised community model gave it 280,000+ volunteer editors creating more breadth and depth than any company could afford to pay for - at a fraction of the cost
Wikipedia focused only on popular topics while Encarta tried to cover everything

questions.explanation: Explanation: Wikipedia's structural advantage is the community model: 280,000+ volunteer editors contributing out of passion and expertise - covering topics in 333 languages with depth no corporate team could replicate. Encarta's professional team, however expert, was limited by salary budget. Wikipedia's Wikimedia Foundation budget (~$100M/year) is tiny compared to what paying 280,000 editors would cost. Scale through community is the insight. Blockchain applies the same principle: validators worldwide contribute to network security in exchange for token rewards - creating a globally distributed, cost-efficient security infrastructure no single company could fund or build.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: C

Explanation: Wikipedia's structural advantage is the community model: 280,000+ volunteer editors contributing out of passion and expertise - covering topics in 333 languages with depth no corporate team could replicate. Encarta's professional team, however expert, was limited by salary budget. Wikipedia's Wikimedia Foundation budget (~$100M/year) is tiny compared to what paying 280,000 editors would cost. Scale through community is the insight. Blockchain applies the same principle: validators worldwide contribute to network security in exchange for token rewards - creating a globally distributed, cost-efficient security infrastructure no single company could fund or build.

questions.text: DHT (Distributed Hash Table) in BitTorrent solved what specific architectural problem - and what is the direct blockchain equivalent?

options.text: DHT speeds up file downloads by compressing data; blockchain equivalent is transaction batching

DHT eliminated the centralised tracker (peer discovery server) by distributing the routing table across all peers; blockchain equivalent is P2P node discovery where all validators collectively maintain network topology without any central directory
DHT verifies file authenticity; blockchain equivalent is the SHA-256 block hash
DHT creates payment for seeders; blockchain equivalent is validator rewards

questions.explanation: Explanation: BitTorrent originally needed a tracker - a central server maintaining a list of who had which files. Trackers were centralised chokepoints: shut one down and swarms could not find peers. DHT solved this by distributing the peer directory itself. Each peer is responsible for storing routing info about files with hashes numerically close to their own peer ID. A query for file hash X gets routed hop-by-hop through DHT peers until it reaches the peer responsible for that hash range. No central server required. Blockchain uses the same Kademlia-style DHT mechanism for node discovery - when you start an MST node, it finds other validators through P2P DHT, not a central server. Both systems solved the same problem: decentralised directory without a central coordinator.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B

Explanation: BitTorrent originally needed a tracker - a central server maintaining a list of who had which files. Trackers were centralised chokepoints: shut one down and swarms could not find peers. DHT solved this by distributing the peer directory itself. Each peer is responsible for storing routing info about files with hashes numerically close to their own peer ID. A query for file hash X gets routed hop-by-hop through DHT peers until it reaches the peer responsible for that hash range. No central server required. Blockchain uses the same Kademlia-style DHT mechanism for node discovery - when you start an MST node, it finds other validators through P2P DHT, not a central server. Both systems solved the same problem: decentralised directory without a central coordinator.

questions.text: BitTorrent technically solved decentralised file distribution, yet popular streaming platforms like Netflix dominate instead of BitTorrent-based alternatives. What is the PRIMARY architectural reason?

options.text: BitTorrent is technically slower than Netflix streaming
BitTorrent is illegal in most countries while Netflix has legitimate content

BitTorrent has no economic incentive for seeders (files become unavailable when people stop sharing), no payment mechanism for content creators, and no discovery or recommendation layer - three gaps blockchain can fill with token economics and smart contracts
BitTorrent requires too much technical knowledge for mainstream users

questions.explanation: Explanation: BitTorrent's technical architecture is sound but its economic architecture is missing. Three specific gaps: (1) Free-rider problem - seeders stop seeding when they're done watching. Rare or old content becomes unavailable. Netflix pays content hosting costs ensuring 100% availability. (2) No creator payment - artists cannot earn from BitTorrent distribution. Netflix pays studios and royalties. (3) No discovery layer - you must know what you're searching for. Netflix's recommendation algorithm surfaces relevant content. Blockchain-based streaming solves all three: token rewards for seeders (economic incentive), smart contract micropayments to creators per view, and on-chain recommendation algorithms. MST Blockchain's ecosystem enables exactly this kind of decentralised content economy.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: C

Explanation: BitTorrent's technical architecture is sound but its economic architecture is missing. Three specific gaps: (1) Free-rider problem - seeders stop seeding when they're done watching. Rare or old content becomes unavailable. Netflix pays content hosting costs ensuring 100% availability. (2) No creator payment - artists cannot earn from BitTorrent distribution. Netflix pays studios and royalties. (3) No discovery layer - you must know what you're searching for. Netflix's recommendation algorithm surfaces relevant content. Blockchain-based streaming solves all three: token rewards for seeders (economic incentive), smart contract micropayments to creators per view, and on-chain recommendation algorithms. MST Blockchain's ecosystem enables exactly this kind of decentralised content economy.

questions.text: Andhra Pradesh's blockchain land records pilot reduced land fraud by 90% in pilot districts. Which specific combination of blockchain properties achieved this - and which Wikipedia-style system alone could NOT have achieved the same result?

options.text: Blockchain's faster processing speed and larger storage capacity compared to a Wikipedia-style system

Blockchain's cryptographic immutability (records cannot be altered without 51%+ validator consensus) COMBINED with cryptographic attribution (private key signatures proving exactly who made each entry) - Wikipedia-style systems still allow admins to delete history and cannot cryptographically prove editor identity
Blockchain's government partnership which Wikipedia-style systems cannot form
Blockchain's ability to store more data than a Wikipedia-style system

questions.explanation: Explanation: A Wikipedia-style system applied to land records would be better than the single-Patwari model (more people watching = harder to corrupt) but would still have two critical vulnerabilities: (1) Admins can delete or modify revision history - a corrupt official who gained admin access could erase evidence of fraudulent changes. (2) Editor identity is not cryptographically verified - a corrupt official could create fake accounts to support fraudulent edits. Blockchain's private key signatures solve both: every entry is signed by a specific private key that only the legitimate official holds, creating mathematical proof of who made the entry. And once confirmed by validators, no record can be altered - not by any admin, not by any official, not by any court order without an on-chain governance process. These two properties together make fraud 90% harder in practice.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B

Explanation: A Wikipedia-style system applied to land records would be better than the single-Patwari model (more people watching = harder to corrupt) but would still have two critical vulnerabilities: (1) Admins can delete or modify revision history - a corrupt official who gained admin access could erase evidence of fraudulent changes. (2) Editor identity is not cryptographically verified - a corrupt official could create fake accounts to support fraudulent edits. Blockchain's private key signatures solve both: every entry is signed by a specific private key that only the legitimate official holds, creating mathematical proof of who made the entry. And once confirmed by validators, no record can be altered - not by any admin, not by any official, not by any court order without an on-chain governance process. These two properties together make fraud 90% harder in practice.

questions.text: When Turkey blocked Wikipedia via ISP-level DNS blocking, Wikipedia's content survived. What does this reveal about the relationship between "access" and "content" in decentralised systems?

options.text: Decentralised systems are completely immune to government interference - Turkey failed entirely

Centralised ISPs can restrict access to content, but if the content is stored outside a government's jurisdiction, they cannot delete it - access and content are two different layers with different centralisation levels
Government DNS blocking is always ineffective because VPNs exist
Wikipedia was centralised enough for Turkey to succeed in removing content about Turkey

questions.explanation: Explanation: The Turkey incident reveals a crucial distinction between two separate layers. The ACCESS layer (how users reach wikipedia.org) was successfully disrupted - Turkish ISPs blocked the DNS name, making the site inaccessible to most users without a VPN. The CONTENT layer (the actual articles) survived completely - Wikipedia's servers are outside Turkey, and Wikimedia Foundation refused to remove the articles the Turkish government objected to. Turkey could control the pipe (centralised ISPs) but not the content (decentralised storage on global servers). This shows that decentralisation works best when applied to BOTH layers: decentralised content (IPFS) eliminates the content SPOF, and P2P access (DHT-based discovery, satellite internet) eliminates the access SPOF. MST Blockchain with IPFS addresses both layers simultaneously.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B

Explanation: The Turkey incident reveals a crucial distinction between two separate layers. The ACCESS layer (how users reach wikipedia.org) was successfully disrupted - Turkish ISPs blocked the DNS name, making the site inaccessible to most users without a VPN. The CONTENT layer (the actual articles) survived completely - Wikipedia's servers are outside Turkey, and Wikimedia Foundation refused to remove the articles the Turkish government objected to. Turkey could control the pipe (centralised ISPs) but not the content (decentralised storage on global servers). This shows that decentralisation works best when applied to BOTH layers: decentralised content (IPFS) eliminates the content SPOF, and P2P access (DHT-based discovery, satellite internet) eliminates the access SPOF. MST Blockchain with IPFS addresses both layers simultaneously.

questions.text: Match each Column A item to its correct Column B equivalent. There is one correct match per item. No item in Column B is used twice.
questions.explanation:
✅ Correct Answers + Full Explanations
1 → B (Governance DAO): Wikipedia's Talk Page is where editors debate contested edits and reach community consensus. The blockchain equivalent is the Governance DAO - where stakeholders propose and vote on protocol changes through structured on-chain deliberation. Both are the "disagreement resolution" mechanism of their respective decentralised systems.
2 → F (Validator): A BitTorrent seeder has the complete file and continuously serves pieces to other users. A blockchain validator has the complete ledger and continuously validates new transactions and blocks. Both are the "always-on participants" who maintain the health of their respective networks.
3 → E (Blockchain Ledger): Wikipedia's edit history preserves every version of every article with timestamps and editor IDs. The blockchain ledger preserves every transaction and state change, permanently and in order. Both are the immutable historical record of their systems - though blockchain's is cryptographically stronger (admins cannot delete entries).
4 → A (Block Hash / Keccak-256): BitTorrent's SHA-1 piece hash verifies that each downloaded piece is uncorrupted and matches what was originally shared. Blockchain's block hash (using stronger SHA-256 or Keccak-256) verifies that each block is uncorrupted and links to the previous block. Both use cryptographic hashing for integrity verification - the core security mechanism of both systems.
5 → D (Slashing Mechanism): Wikipedia's bots automatically detect and revert vandalism without human intervention. Blockchain's slashing mechanism automatically penalises (reduces staked tokens) of validators who submit invalid blocks - without requiring human review. Both are the automated, real-time defence against bad-faith participation.
6 → C (P2P Node Discovery): BitTorrent DHT distributes the peer directory across all nodes - no central tracker needed to find who has a file. Blockchain P2P node discovery (also often Kademlia-based DHT) distributes network topology information across all validators - no central server needed to find other nodes. Same mechanism, different application.
6/6: All six correct matches
4 - 5/6: Four to five correct matches
2 - 3/6: Two to three correct matches
0 - 1/6: Zero to one correct match

questions.text: Statement: "Wikipedia's decentralised model is superior to blockchain in all respects because it has been operating successfully since 2001 without cryptocurrency or tokens."
questions.explanation: Wikipedia's community governance model is excellent for knowledge curation but it cannot transfer value trustlessly (no payment layer), cannot guarantee cryptographic immutability (admins can delete history), cannot cryptographically attribute entries to specific identities, cannot automatically reward contributors economically (free-rider problem causes editor burnout), and cannot execute programmable business logic - all of which blockchain provides; the statement confuses operational longevity with architectural completeness across all dimensions, whereas Wikipedia and blockchain are complementary tools designed for different tasks, with blockchain uniquely solving the economic incentive and trustless value transfer problems that have always limited Wikipedia's sustainability and contributor diversity.
questions.modelAnswer:
FALSE
✅ Required Justification (1 Mark)

Wikipedia's community governance model is excellent for knowledge curation but it cannot transfer value trustlessly (no payment layer), cannot guarantee cryptographic immutability (admins can delete history), cannot cryptographically attribute entries to specific identities, cannot automatically reward contributors economically (free-rider problem causes editor burnout), and cannot execute programmable business logic - all of which blockchain provides; the statement confuses operational longevity with architectural completeness across all dimensions, whereas Wikipedia and blockchain are complementary tools designed for different tasks, with blockchain uniquely solving the economic incentive and trustless value transfer problems that have always limited Wikipedia's sustainability and contributor diversity.
